activity_metadata.objective: Students will be able to explain how specific events and structural elements contribute to the development of theme and plot in a narrative text.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the "Your Turn" section of the Story Structure Skill lesson. It provides a focused analysis of how a specific passage contributes to story structure and theme development.
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity supports the Extended Writing Project where students write a narrative about a character facing an unexpected challenge. Students can use "Scout's Honor" as a mentor text, applying their understanding of how authors structure challenging moments and use environmental elements to create tension and develop themes about testing one's limits.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This skills lesson supports the close read lesson and connects to the unit essential question "What do we do when life gets hard?" by analyzing how the author structures a passage where the boys face multiple challenges that test their limits and reveal their unpreparedness.
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Excerpt from “Scout’s Honor” by Avi, paragraphs 80-98

80 "I think we're lost," I announced.

81 "What do we do?" Horse whispered. His voice was jagged with panic. That made me feel better.

82 "Don't worry," Max said. "I've got my compass." He pulled it out. "North is that way," he said, pointing in the direction we had been going.

83 Horse said, "You sure?"

84 "A Scout compass never lies," Max insisted.

85 "We lied," I reminded him.

86 "Yeah, but this is an official Scout compass," Max returned loyally.

87 "Come on," Max said and marched forward. Horse and I followed. In moments, we crossed a metal bar on the walkway. On one side, a sign proclaimed: NEW YORK; on the other, it said: NEW JERSEY.

88 "Holy smoke," Horse said with reverence as he straddled the bar. "Talk about being tough. We're in two states at the same time."

89 It began to rain. Max said, "Maybe it'll keep us clean."

90 "You saying I'm not clean?" Horse shot back.

91 Ever friendly, I put up my umbrella.

92 We went on---Max on one side, Horse on the other, me in the middle---trying to avoid the growing puddles. After a while, Max said, "Would you move the umbrella? Rain is coming down my neck."

93 "We're supposed to be roughing it," I said.

94 "Being in the middle isn't roughing it," Horse reminded me.

95 I folded the umbrella up so we could all get soaked equally.

96 "Hey!" I cried. "Look!" Staring up ahead, I could make out tollbooths and the dim outlines of buildings.

97 "Last one off the bridge is a rotten egg!" Horse shouted and began to run. The next second, he tripped and took off like an F-36 fighter plane. Unfortunately, he landed like a Hell-cat dive-bomber as his mattress unspooled before him and then slammed into a big puddle.

98 Max and I ran to help. Horse was damp. His mattress was soaked. When he tried to roll it up, water cascaded like Niagara Falls.
